items.q: What was the Tet Offensive's main significance?
items.correct: Militarily the Viet Cong suffered heavy losses, but politically it shattered the US public belief that the war was being won, fuelling anti-war pressure on Johnson.
items.misconception: The Tet Offensive was a military victory for the Viet Cong that drove out the US.
items.random_wrong: The Tet Offensive ended the Vietnam War in 1968.
items.why: Militarily it was a US tactical win but a strategic political loss. Students confuse battlefield outcomes with political effects — vital for understanding insurgency warfare.

items.q: Why was the Berlin Wall built in 1961?
items.correct: To stop the mass flow of East Germans to the West via Berlin (millions had fled), which was draining the GDR of skilled workers and threatening the regime's survival.
items.misconception: The Berlin Wall was built to stop a Western invasion of East Berlin.
items.random_wrong: The Berlin Wall was built by NATO to keep East Germans out of West Germany.
items.why: The Wall faced inward to trap GDR citizens, not outward against invasion. Students miss that it was an admission of weakness — people were voting with their feet.

items.q: Why did Stalin blockade West Berlin in 1948?
items.correct: To force the Western Allies out of Berlin in response to the merging of their zones, introduction of the Deutschmark, and creation of a separate West German state.
items.misconception: Stalin blockaded Berlin because he wanted to invade Western Europe.
items.random_wrong: Stalin blockaded Berlin to stop Nazis escaping to the West.
items.why: The blockade was reactive, not a step toward invasion. Students see all Soviet actions as offensive; in fact Berlin was a defensive move against Western consolidation.

items.q: When was the Berlin Wall constructed?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 13 August 1961. “9 November 1989” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which agreement ended direct US combat in Vietnam?
items.why: Correct answer: Paris Peace Accords 1973. “Geneva Accords 1954” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many days did the Cuban Missile Crisis last?
items.why: Exact answer: 13. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
